entries.definition: Slang for testicles; also a dismissive interjection meaning 'nonsense'.
entries.etymology: Old English 'beallucas' (testicles); by the 1860s meant 'nonsense'; Sex Pistols 1977.

entries.definition: The buttocks; also a fool.
entries.etymology: Old English 'ærs' — cognate with Dutch 'aars', German 'Arsch'.

entries.definition: Originally sodomite; now a mild exclamation or term for an annoying person.
entries.etymology: From Medieval Latin 'Bulgarus' — referring to heretical Bulgarian sect; meaning shifted through centuries.

entries.definition: British intensifier conveying emphasis or mild exasperation.
entries.etymology: Likely from 'by our Lady'; disputed. Mild UK intensifier.

entries.definition: Expression of surprise, contracted from 'God blind me'.
entries.etymology: Contraction of 'God blind me!' — minced oath.

entries.definition: The buttocks; also a loafer.
entries.etymology: Dates to 14th C Middle English — probably onomatopoeic.

entries.definition: Slang for testicles.
entries.etymology: From 'knacker' — a horse slaughterer; slang for testicles since 19th C.

entries.definition: Exclamation of astonishment, euphemism for 'Christ'.
entries.etymology: Minced oath — euphemism for 'Christ'.

entries.definition: Polite substitute for a stronger s-word; also the sweetener.
entries.etymology: Stand-in for a stronger s-word; established 20th C.

entries.definition: Mild substitute for a stronger f-word; also the confection.
entries.etymology: Euphemism for a stronger f-word; also confection since 19th C.

entries.definition: Polite American substitute for 'shit'.
entries.etymology: American euphemism for s-word; 19th C onwards.

entries.definition: Mild form of 'damn' used as an exclamation or intensifier.
entries.etymology: Minced form of 'damn'; 18th C.

entries.definition: Self-deprecating or mildly exasperated exclamation.
entries.etymology: Originally 'husks of no value' → worthless → mild exclamation.

entries.definition: Dismissive exclamation of disdain or disgust.
entries.etymology: From Yiddish 'feh'; popularised 1920s.

entries.definition: Mild exclamation of frustration or disappointment.
entries.etymology: Mild exclamation of frustration.

entries.definition: Mild British exclamation of surprise.
entries.etymology: Minced form of 'Christ' via 'cripes'.

entries.definition: Mild exclamation of annoyance, from 'God rot'.
entries.etymology: Contraction of 'od rot' — mild minced oath.

entries.definition: Nonsense or worthless talk.
entries.etymology: From Dutch 'pappekak' — soft dung. Polite now, very rude in origin.

entries.definition: Senseless talk or writing; nonsense.
entries.etymology: Originally 16th C for a frothy drink; meaning 'nonsense' by 17th C.

entries.definition: British term for utter nonsense.
entries.etymology: Disputed origin; possibly from Hiram Codd's bottle + 'wallop' (beer).

entries.definition: British slang for nonsense or rubbish.
entries.etymology: 19th C slang for rubbish; origin disputed.

entries.definition: Exclamation dismissing something as nonsense.
entries.etymology: From violin bows being flimsy and trivial; 17th C.

entries.definition: Worthless talk; rubbish; originally pig feed.
entries.etymology: Originally slop fed to pigs; figurative by 18th C.

entries.definition: Nonsense; empty or foolish talk.
entries.etymology: 1920s US slang; possibly from Russian/Yiddish.

entries.definition: Trivial or foolish speech or writing.
entries.etymology: Late 18th C, from 'twattle' (prattle).

entries.definition: Empty political talk; insincere speechmaking.
entries.etymology: From Buncombe County, NC — a congressman's 1820 tedious speech.

entries.definition: Deceptive or pretentious talk; nonsense.
entries.etymology: Mid-18th C; made famous by Dickens' Scrooge.

entries.definition: Pretentious nonsense designed to win applause.
entries.etymology: Originally a theatrical device to 'trap claps' from the audience.

entries.definition: Scots term for a foolish or ineffectual person.
entries.etymology: Scots, late 20th C; origin uncertain — possibly from 'numps' (fool).

entries.definition: British slang for a silly or foolish person.
entries.etymology: 1960s UK slang; disputed origin — possibly from 'Walter'.

entries.definition: British slang for a fool; originally vulgar for penis.
entries.etymology: Originally slang for penis (1940s); meaning 'fool' from 1980s sitcoms.

entries.definition: A silly or annoyingly foolish person.
entries.etymology: From 'twit' (to taunt); as 'fool' from 1920s RAF slang.

entries.definition: British slang for an incompetent or naive person.
entries.etymology: 1980s UK slang from Jim Henson's Muppets; gentle insult.

entries.definition: A stupid or silly person.
entries.etymology: 1920s, from 'nit' (nothing) + wit.

entries.definition: British slang for a fool, via Cockney rhyming slang.
entries.etymology: Cockney rhyming slang: Berkshire Hunt → c**t. Most users don't know origin.

entries.definition: British slang for a foolish person.
entries.etymology: Originally northern English for penis; meaning 'fool' from mid-20th C.

entries.definition: Archaic oath expressing surprise, from 'God's wounds'.
entries.etymology: Contraction of 'God's wounds' — 16th C oath.

entries.definition: Archaic mild oath, from 'God's hooks'.
entries.etymology: 'God's hooks' — referring to nails of the crucifixion.

entries.definition: Archaic exclamation, euphemism for 'Oh God'.
entries.etymology: Euphemism for 'Oh God'; 17th C.

entries.definition: Minced form of 'Christ' used as an exclamation.
entries.etymology: Minced form of 'Christ'; late 19th C.

entries.definition: Archaic term for a menial servant or a rogue.
entries.etymology: Medieval: a knight's attendant → rogue by 16th C.

entries.definition: An unprincipled, untrustworthy, or dishonest man.
entries.etymology: Old English 'cnafa' — boy; meaning 'rogue' by 13th C.

entries.definition: A mischievous rogue or rascal.
entries.etymology: 17th C alteration of 'rascal'.

entries.definition: A dishonest or unprincipled person.
entries.etymology: Origin disputed; 16th C.

entries.definition: A charlatan; a fraudulent showman or quack.
entries.etymology: From Italian 'monta in banco' — one who climbs on a bench (to sell fake cures).

entries.definition: An unscrupulous, shrewd politician.
entries.etymology: 19th C US — a shrewd, unprincipled politician.

entries.definition: Hiberno-English pronunciation of 'idiot'.
entries.etymology: Hiberno-English rendering of 'idiot'.

entries.definition: Irish mild form of the f-word, used as an intensifier.
entries.etymology: Softened form of the f-word; popularised by Father Ted.

entries.definition: Scots slang for a crazy or foolish person.
entries.etymology: Scots for 'crazy person'; origin disputed.

entries.definition: Irish slang for someone who talks foolish nonsense.
entries.etymology: Irish slang: 'gob' (mouth) + 'shite'; someone who talks nonsense.

entries.definition: Australian slang for a fool or hopeless case.
entries.etymology: From an Australian racehorse (1920s) that never won; then bird → fool.

entries.definition: Australian slang for a loud fool; also a pink cockatoo.
entries.etymology: From the Aboriginal word for a noisy pink cockatoo.

entries.definition: Australian/NZ slang for an unrefined or boorish person.
entries.etymology: AU/NZ for an unrefined person; popularised 1980s.

entries.definition: Strong British/Australian insult for a contemptible person.
entries.etymology: Strong insult in UK/AU; anatomical origin. Medium in AU, stronger in UK.

entries.definition: Fictional profanity from 'Battlestar Galactica'.
entries.etymology: Battlestar Galactica (1978 / 2004) — invented profanity.

entries.definition: Fictional profanity from the UK sitcom 'Red Dwarf'.
entries.etymology: Red Dwarf (1988) — invented profanity.

entries.definition: Fictional profanity from the TV series 'Farscape'.
entries.etymology: Farscape (1999) — invented profanity.

entries.definition: Fictional minced oath from the TV series 'Firefly'.
entries.etymology: Firefly (2002) — fictional minced 'goddamn'.

entries.definition: Derogatory term for a greedy or contemptible person.
entries.etymology: Derogatory use since 14th C.

entries.definition: British slang (often for a woman) considered disagreeable.
entries.etymology: Used of women since 17th C — gendered; review case by case.

entries.definition: Mild oath or intensifier; historically a religious condemnation.
entries.etymology: From Old French 'damner' → to condemn; softened to a general intensifier.

entries.definition: American softened form of 'damn'.
entries.etymology: Southern US minced form of 'damn'; 18th C.

entries.definition: Softened form of 'hell'.
entries.etymology: Minced form of 'hell'; late 19th C.

entries.definition: Minced form of 'Jesus' used as an exclamation.
entries.etymology: Minced form of 'Jesus'; popularised 1920s US.

entries.definition: Mild exclamation, euphemism for 'Jesus'.
entries.etymology: Euphemism for 'Jesus Christ'; 1920s US.

entries.definition: Mild oath used as a substitute for 'God'.
entries.etymology: Minced form of 'God'; 18th C.

entries.definition: Mild exclamation, euphemism for 'God'.
entries.etymology: Euphemism for 'God'; 18th C.

entries.definition: Mild exclamation, clipped from 'Jesus'.
entries.etymology: Clipped form of 'Jesus'; late 19th C US.

entries.definition: Vulgar word for excrement; also trivial rubbish or nonsense.
entries.etymology: Middle English 'crappe' (chaff, residue); allegedly popularised alongside Thomas Crapper the plumber.

entries.definition: Accumulated dirt; low-grade rubbish.
entries.etymology: 1940s US GI slang; back-formation or metathesis of 'curd'.

entries.definition: Something outstanding, impressive, or difficult.
entries.etymology: Early 20th C US; possibly from actress Eleanora Duse or Duesenberg cars.

entries.definition: Insincere or pretentious talk; nonsense.
entries.etymology: 1920s US; origin disputed — possibly from Irish surname.

entries.definition: Nonsense; foolish talk.
entries.etymology: From Bologna sausage; meaning 'nonsense' by 1920s US.

entries.definition: Expression of dismissive disbelief; nonsense.
entries.etymology: 1920s US; coined by cartoonist Billy DeBeck as a minced euphemism.

entries.definition: American slang for nonsense or rubbish.
entries.etymology: Late 19th C US; possibly from French 'bois de vache' — dried cow dung.

entries.definition: Rural American minced oath for 'goddamn it'.
entries.etymology: Ozark/Southern US minced form of 'God damn it'.

entries.definition: American mild oath, blend of 'eternal damnation'.
entries.etymology: 18th C US; blend of 'eternal' and 'damnation'.

entries.definition: Dated American mild oath, euphemism for 'damn'.
entries.etymology: 19th C US euphemism for 'concern' / 'damn'.

entries.definition: Exclamation of surprise, short for 'for heaven's sakes'.
entries.etymology: Regional US — 'sakes alive', 'for Pete's sake'.

entries.definition: Mild American exclamation, minced form of 'Christ'.
entries.etymology: Mid-19th C US; minced form of 'Christ'.

entries.definition: American exclamation of surprise.
entries.etymology: 1920s US sportscaster catchphrase (Harry Caray, Phil Rizzuto).

entries.definition: American exclamation of astonishment.
entries.etymology: 1920s US; later popularised by Captain Marvel comics.

entries.definition: American exclamation of surprise or alarm.
entries.etymology: Early 20th C US; euphemism for 'holy smoke of incense'.

entries.definition: American exclamation of surprise.
entries.etymology: Early 20th C US; possibly anti-Catholic origin (Friday fish).

entries.definition: Mild American oath for a rascal or surprising event.
entries.etymology: Naval origin — children born aboard ship near the guns.

entries.definition: Trivial nonsense or petty fussing.
entries.etymology: 17th C English, common in 19th C US; reduplication of 'fiddle'.

entries.definition: An incompetent or foolish person.
entries.etymology: 1960s US campus slang; origin uncertain.

entries.definition: An unfashionably studious or socially awkward person.
entries.etymology: 1960s US college slang; likely blend of 'dwarf' and 'feeb'.

entries.definition: A silly, socially inept person.
entries.etymology: 1960s US; originally slang for penis, now chiefly 'fool'.

entries.definition: A foolish or unsophisticated person; a peanut.
entries.etymology: From Gullah for 'peanut'; as 'fool' chiefly US Southern 20th C.

entries.definition: A slow-witted or stupid person.
entries.etymology: Mid-19th C US; 'lump' + 'head'.

entries.definition: A stupid or foolish person.
entries.etymology: 1940s US; popularised by Moe Howard of the Three Stooges.

entries.definition: A dim-witted or thuggish person.
entries.etymology: 1940s US; popularised by 'All in the Family' (1970s).

entries.definition: Contemptible or foolish person; Yiddish-origin insult.
entries.etymology: From Yiddish 'shmok' — penis; Yiddish origin keeps it strong in-community.
entries.notes: Yiddish origin; some speakers find stronger

entries.definition: A fool; Yiddish-origin insult.
entries.etymology: From Yiddish 'pots' — penis; meaning 'fool' in US English.

entries.definition: A clumsy, awkward person.
entries.etymology: From Yiddish 'klots' — block of wood; clumsy person.

entries.definition: A fool; originally an epithet for a great hunter.
entries.etymology: Biblical hunter; inverted to 'fool' by Bugs Bunny's mocking use (1940s).
entries.notes: HERO WORD — feature prominently in early puzzles. Bugs Bunny etymology is a showcase card.

entries.definition: A rude, loud, or boorish person.
entries.etymology: Coined by Jonathan Swift (Gulliver's Travels, 1726); US sense: boorish person.

entries.definition: Absolutely nothing; worthless amount.
entries.etymology: Yiddish-origin for 'nothing of value'; literally 'goat droppings'.

entries.definition: Mild exclamation of surprise, euphemism for 'Jesus'.
entries.etymology: Euphemism for 'Jesus'; popularised by Disney's Pinocchio (1940).

entries.definition: American minced oath for 'Jesus Christ'.
entries.etymology: Modern US Christian-friendly euphemism for 'Jesus Christ'.

entries.definition: Used as a mild intensifier in phrases like 'scared the bejesus'.
entries.etymology: Irish-American; 'by Jesus'; usually in 'scared the bejesus out of'.

entries.definition: Intensifier invoking the torments of hell.
entries.etymology: Southern US; intensifier of 'hell'.

entries.definition: A toilet; also a contemptible person.
entries.etymology: Popularly (and incorrectly) attributed to Thomas Crapper, 19th C plumber; actually earlier.

entries.definition: A trivial person or thing; especially in 'peanut gallery'.
entries.etymology: 'Peanut gallery' = cheap seats for hecklers; vaudeville-era.

entries.definition: A failure; a foolish or inept person.
entries.etymology: 1950s US; a flop or a fool.

entries.definition: A stupid or stubborn person.
entries.etymology: 1903 US baseball slang; popularised by Fred Merkle's 'boner' play.

entries.definition: A clumsy or slow-witted person.
entries.etymology: 19th C US; 'chuck' = lump of wood.

entries.definition: A silly or eccentric person.
entries.etymology: Late 19th C US; popularised by Archie Bunker ('All in the Family').

entries.definition: A foolish or stupid person.
entries.etymology: 1960s US; mechanical and anatomical double meaning.

entries.definition: Southern American verb meaning 'suppose' or 'think'.
entries.etymology: Old English 'ge-recenian' — to recount. Southern US retains 'I reckon' where other dialects lost it.

entries.definition: Askew, crooked, or in disarray.
entries.etymology: Mid-19th C US; 'askew, crooked, all wrong' — Southern and Appalachian.

entries.definition: Ridiculous, implausible, or nonsensical.
entries.etymology: 1940s NYC children's slang — corruption of 'decalcomania' (transfer tattoos) meaning flimsy/fake.

entries.definition: American mild exclamation of surprise.
entries.etymology: 19th C US; the 'Betsy' may be Betsy Ross, but nobody's sure.

entries.definition: Southern American mild exclamation of shock.
entries.etymology: Southern US exclamation; truncated religious plea.

entries.definition: Truncated exclamation of surprise from 'I'll be damned'.
entries.etymology: Truncated 'I'll be damned'; Southern US.

entries.definition: Bound hand and foot; helplessly restricted.
entries.etymology: Cowboy origin — to tie a hog's legs; 'stuck, restrained'.

entries.definition: To cheat, deceive, or swindle.
entries.etymology: Early 19th C US frontier slang; 'to cheat or swindle'.

entries.definition: Nonsense; foolish talk.
entries.etymology: Mid-19th C US; nonsense, drivel.

entries.definition: A stupid or obnoxious person; also a donkey.
entries.etymology: From 'jack' (male) + 'ass' (donkey); insult use 17th C.

entries.definition: Midwestern interjection of mild apology or surprise.
entries.etymology: Midwestern 'oh, excuse me' — viral meme-famous from ~2015.
entries.notes: Very on-brand for a puzzle category

entries.definition: Norwegian-American exclamation of dismay or effort.
entries.etymology: Minnesotan/Norwegian-American expression of dismay or effort.

entries.definition: American exclamation of exasperation.
entries.etymology: Minced form of 'for Christ's sake'; 1920s US.

entries.definition: Mild American exclamation of exasperation.
entries.etymology: 'Pete' is a stand-in for 'Peter' / 'God'; 1910s US.

entries.definition: Rhyming intensifier of 'jeez' expressing surprise.
entries.etymology: Rhyming intensifier of 'jeez'; mid-20th C US.

entries.definition: Upper Midwest conversational tag meaning 'you know'.
entries.etymology: Upper Midwest tag phrase; 'Fargo' (1996) made it iconic.

entries.definition: New England intensifier meaning 'very'.
entries.etymology: New England intensifier ('wicked good'); regional since 19th C.

entries.definition: An eccentric or crazy person.
entries.etymology: 1950s US surfer/beatnik slang; 'crazy person'.

entries.definition: A mediocre or clumsy boxer; a stupid lout.
entries.etymology: 1920s US boxing slang — a mediocre fighter; origin disputed.

entries.definition: A chaotic situation; military acronym for 'Situation Normal, All Fouled Up'.
entries.etymology: WWII US military acronym: Situation Normal, All F***ed Up.

entries.definition: Completely ruined; military acronym for 'Fouled Up Beyond All Recognition'.
entries.etymology: WWII US military acronym: F***ed Up Beyond All Recognition.
entries.notes: Stronger than SNAFU — note in card

entries.definition: Resigned military acronym for 'Bend Over, Here It Comes Again'.
entries.etymology: US military acronym: Bend Over, Here It Comes Again.

entries.definition: WWII military acronym for 'Things Are Really Fouled Up'.
entries.etymology: WWII US military acronym: Things Are Really F***ed Up.

entries.definition: A confusing, chaotic, or poorly run operation.
entries.etymology: US military/aviation slang for a chaotic situation.

entries.definition: A chaotic mess; minced form of stronger military slang.
entries.etymology: Minced form of stronger military term; 'a chaotic mess'.

entries.definition: Military phonetic euphemism for 'cluster-f***'.
entries.etymology: NATO phonetic for C.F. — a 'cluster' of the military kind.

entries.definition: A hapless, downtrodden loser.
entries.etymology: WWII cartoon by George Baker; 'a hapless loser'.

entries.definition: Military slang for a non-combat soldier or shirker.
entries.etymology: Modern US military slang for a non-combat soldier.

entries.definition: Bad, inferior, or nonsensical.
entries.etymology: 1980s US hip-hop; 'bad, inferior'.

entries.definition: Not genuine; counterfeit or fake.
entries.etymology: 1790s US counterfeit coins; 'fake, bad'.

entries.definition: Ruined, broken, or ugly.
entries.etymology: 1990s US slang; ugly or broken.

entries.definition: Bitter, resentful, or upset about something.
entries.etymology: 2010s US internet slang; 'bitter or resentful'. Earlier Navy slang too.

entries.definition: Acting irrationally or overreacting.
entries.etymology: 1960s US drug slang → 1990s hip-hop; 'acting irrationally'.

entries.definition: Startled, shocked, or deeply rattled.
entries.etymology: 2010s US hip-hop → internet slang; 'startled, shocked'.

entries.definition: To show disrespect through insult or mockery.
entries.etymology: 1980s US hip-hop; clipped 'disrespect'.

entries.definition: Of poor quality; shoddy or unreliable.
entries.etymology: 2000s US slang; 'shoddy, unreliable'.

entries.definition: Southern phrase whose pity often masks contempt.
entries.etymology: Southern US — sweet on the surface, devastating underneath. The passive-aggressive masterpiece.
